1: Numbers 5 opens a sequence of laws concerning purity (v1-4), restitution (v5-10), and marital fidelity (v11-31) — the social and ritual conditions for maintaining the holy camp. Chapter 4 organized the carriers of holiness; chapter 5 protects the space that holiness inhabits. The divine speech formula (vayedabber YHWH el Moshe lemor) is the standard narrative frame that introduces each new legislative unit.
2: tsarua venaza: tsara'at (skin disease, traditionally rendered 'leprosy' but not necessarily Hansen's disease) and zav (a person with a bodily discharge) — two of the three categories of ritual impurity required to be expelled from the camp. The third is tame lanafesh (corpse-impurity). All three are detailed in Lev 13-15. The three categories represent the major vectors of ritual impurity in the Levitical system: contagious skin conditions, bodily emissions, and death-contact.
3: lemaan lo yetamme'u et machaneihem asher ani shoken betocham: 'that they may not defile the camp where I dwell.' The theological rationale for the expulsion is not hygiene but holiness — YHWH's presence dwells (shochen) in the camp, and the impure and the holy cannot coexist. The verb shaken (dwell/tabernacle) is the root of mishkan (tabernacle/dwelling). YHWH's tabernacling presence is the ground of both the command to expel the impure and the promise to Israel that YHWH is among them.
4: Israel's obedience is briefly noted: 'The Israelites did so and put them outside the camp, just as YHWH had spoken to Moses.' The formulaic compliance statement (keasher diber YHWH el Moshe) confirms faithful execution of the divine command. In Numbers' narrative structure, such compliance statements often precede a significant failure or complaint — this brief positive note before the sotah section is notable.
5: A second divine address introduces the restitution law (v5-10). This law bridges the purity concerns of v1-4 (ritual integrity of the camp) and the relational integrity of the community (v11-31). Defilement of the camp's ritual purity requires physical expulsion; defilement of social trust requires material restitution and confession.
6: ish o ishah ki ya'asu mikol chatot ha'adam lima'ol ma'al baYHWH: 'When a man or woman commits any of the sins that people commit, acting in breach of faith against YHWH.' The framing is theologically significant: sins against a neighbor are simultaneously sins against YHWH (lima'ol ma'al = to act treacherously/commit a trespass). Ma'al is a covenantal term for breach of trust with the covenant partner — the interpersonal sin is an act of covenant disloyalty to YHWH himself.
7: vehitvaddu et chata'tam asher asu: 'that person shall confess the sin committed.' Confession (hitvaddah, Hithpael of yadah) here means explicit verbal acknowledgment — not merely feeling guilt but speaking the sin aloud, presumably before the priest and/or the wronged party. The restitution follows immediately: repayment of the principal plus one-fifth (chamishit). The 20% surcharge (same as in Leviticus 5:16, Numbers 6:5) makes restitution more than symbolic — it creates a material incentive for honest dealing over fraudulent concealment.
8: im ein la'ish goel lehashiv ha'asham elav: 'if the wronged person has no relative (go'el) to receive the restitution.' The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) is the one who receives restitution on behalf of a wronged party who has died. If no go'el exists, the restitution goes to YHWH via the priest, along with the ram of atonement. This provision prevents restitution from becoming legally impossible when the wronged party is deceased without heirs — YHWH himself receives what no human can claim.
9: kol terumah lekhol qadshe beney Yisrael: every sacred donation that the Israelites bring to the priest belongs to the priest. The generalization appended to the restitution law explains the principle behind v8: the priest receives what belongs to YHWH because the priest is YHWH's representative. The restitution to YHWH-via-priest is not confiscation but proper routing through the ordained mediator.
10: The principle of priestly receipt of holy gifts is stated axiomatically: whatever a man gives to the priest is the priest's. The statement reinforces that sacred donations are irrevocable — once given to YHWH, they cannot be recalled. This is the legal foundation for the Levitical support system: the Levites have no land inheritance because YHWH is their inheritance, mediated through the gifts of the people (Numbers 18:20-21).
11: The third divine address in chapter 5 introduces the sotah ritual (the 'law of jealousy,' v11-31) — the most complex and controversial legal procedure in Numbers. The sotah addresses a specific evidentiary problem: a husband suspects his wife of adultery but there are no witnesses. The law provides a judicial ordeal that resolves the suspicion without requiring impossible evidence.
12: ish asher tisteh ishto uma'alah vo ma'al: 'if a man's wife goes astray (satah) and acts treacherously (ma'al) against him.' Satah (to deviate/go astray) is the name-source for the ritual (sotah). Ma'al again appears as the covenant-disloyalty term — unfaithfulness to a husband is the same breach-of-trust language used for covenant unfaithfulness to YHWH. The marriage covenant is the human analogue to the divine-human covenant; adultery is covenant treason at the domestic level.
13: The scenario: the husband has no witnesses and she was not caught in the act. The evidentiary problem is real — Leviticus 20:10 required two witnesses for an adultery charge; without them, no execution is possible. The sotah ritual fills this legal gap. It is important that the ritual requires the husband's suspicion, not certainty — it is specifically designed for the situation of unresolvable human uncertainty, where only divine knowledge can provide a verdict.
14: ruach qin'ah: 'a spirit of jealousy comes over him.' Qin'ah is the same word used for YHWH's own jealousy (El qanna', Exodus 20:5) — a passionate, exclusive devotion that does not tolerate divided loyalty. The husband's jealousy mirrors YHWH's: both are the response of a covenant partner to suspected infidelity. The marriage-covenant jealousy theme will resonate in the prophets' use of adultery-metaphor for Israel's idolatry (Hos 2; Ezek 16).
15: The husband brings the suspected wife to the priest with a 'grain offering of jealousy' (mincha qin'ah) — barley flour without oil or incense. The absence of oil and incense distinguishes this offering from standard grain offerings (which have both, Leviticus 2:1) and aligns it with sin-related grain offerings (Leviticus 5:11: 'He shall put no oil on it and put no frankincense on it, for it is a sin offering'). The offering's character communicates the occasion: this is not a celebration but an investigation into potential sin.
16: veheqriv otah hakohen vehe'emidah lifney YHWH: 'the priest shall bring her forward and present her before YHWH.' The woman's standing 'before YHWH' at the sanctuary entrance is the juridical-religious posture for divine judgment. The priest acts as both judicial officer and religious functionary — the sotah ritual is simultaneously a legal procedure and a divine ordeal, with YHWH as the ultimate witness and judge.
17: mayim qedoshim bikli cheres: 'holy water in a clay vessel.' The water is taken from the bronze laver — the kiyyor — the water of the sanctuary. Dust from the floor of the tabernacle is added. The mixture of sanctuary water and tabernacle dust creates a substance that is neither purely natural nor purely sacred — it occupies a liminal space appropriate for a liminal judgment. The clay vessel (keli cheres) is the most ordinary container, contrasting with the extraordinary nature of the ordeal.
18: ufara et rosh ha'ishah: 'the priest shall let down [literally: uncover/loosen] the woman's hair.' Hair-uncovering in the ancient world was a mark of mourning or humiliation. The visual act publicly marks the woman as under suspicion — not yet condemned but exposed to the community's gaze. The grain offering of jealousy is placed in her hands: she holds the evidence of the charge against her while the curse-waters are prepared.
19: The priest administers the shevu'at alah (oath of cursing): 'If no man has lain with you, and if you have not gone astray into uncleanness while under your husband's authority, be free from this water of bitterness that brings the curse.' The oath has a double structure: negative condition (if innocent) → freedom; positive condition (if guilty) → curse. The oath-structure is the legal mechanism that activates the ordeal: the woman's own spoken response (amen, amen) consents to the divine verdict.
20: The positive/guilt condition: 'but if you have gone astray while under your husband's authority, if you have defiled yourself and some man other than your husband has lain with you.' The phrase tachat isha (under a husband, i.e., under his authority) is the legal term for the married state. The adultery is framed as defilement (nitmeit) — not merely behavioral transgression but ritual-covenantal contamination.
21: yiten YHWH otach le'alah velishvu'ah betoch ammech: 'May YHWH make you a curse and an oath among your people.' If guilty, the woman becomes a public example of the consequence of adultery — a living object-lesson for the community. The curse-formula uses two technical terms: alah (curse/oath) and shevuah (oath) — she will become what others invoke when they want to describe the fate of the unfaithful. The physical symptoms (swollen abdomen, failed womb) are the visible mark of divine judgment.
22: uva'u hamayim hame'arerim ha'eleh beme'ayich latsbot beten velanefil yarekh: 'May this water that brings the curse go into your body to cause your abdomen to swell and your womb to fail.' The physical consequences — distended abdomen (tsevet beten) and failed womb (nefal yarekh) — are the bodily manifestation of covenant-curse. The womb-failure is particularly significant given that the Abrahamic covenant was carried through offspring: to lose the capacity for childbearing is to be cut off from the covenantal promise in its most physical dimension.
23: vekatav et ha'alot haeleh hakohen basefer umachah el mey hamaririm: 'The priest shall write these curses in a scroll and wash them off into the bitter water.' This is the most striking detail of the entire ritual: the written curse is washed off into the water, and the woman drinks the curses. The word, once written, becomes physically embodied in the water she drinks. This 'word-made-liquid' logic is unique in the biblical ritual system — perhaps the most direct form of oath-consumption in the ancient Near East.
24: vehishqah et ha'ishah et mey hamaririm hame'arerim: 'He shall make the woman drink the bitter water that brings the curse.' The woman drinks the divine verdict: if guilty, the curse written in the scroll and dissolved into the water acts through her body; if innocent, it passes through her harmlessly. The ordeal is not about the natural properties of the water — it is about divine response to the drinking of a written oath. YHWH is the active agent of whatever verdict follows.
25: The priest performs the wave offering (henipha) of the grain offering before YHWH, then burns a handful as the memorial portion (azkaratah) on the altar. The azkarah (memorial/token portion) is the part of the grain offering that goes to YHWH; the rest belongs to the priest (Leviticus 2:9). In the sotah procedure, the wave-and-burn of the memorial portion is the sacrificial action that accompanies the ordeal — the grain offering activates the divine attention before the woman drinks.
26: After burning the memorial portion, the priest makes the woman drink the water. The sequence — oath, hair-loosening, grain offering in hands, writing curses, washing into water, wave offering, burn memorial portion, drink — is elaborate and irreversible. The public, multi-step character of the procedure is itself a significant deterrent: the humiliation of the process makes false accusation socially costly for the husband as well.
27: im nitme'ah vatim'ol ma'al be'ishah: 'if she has defiled herself and acted treacherously against her husband.' The guilt-outcome: the water activates the curse, her abdomen swells, her womb fails, and 'she shall be a curse among her people.' The physical consequences are divine judgment, not the water's pharmacology. The ritual assumes that YHWH actively intervenes to execute judgment — the ordeal only works because YHWH is the enforcer of the covenant, and the marriage covenant violation has already been declared a breach against YHWH (v6).
28: ve'im lo nitme'ah ha'ishah utehorah hi veniqtah venizre'ah zara: 'But if the woman has not defiled herself and is clean, she will be free from harm and will be able to conceive.' The innocence-outcome is striking: not merely 'she is declared not guilty' but 'she shall be able to conceive children.' The positive result — fertility — is the specific reversal of the guilt-curse (womb-failure, v22). Innocence is vindicated through the restoration of the covenantal blessing most directly threatened by the curse. YHWH's verdict through the ordeal blesses the innocent in the very area the guilty would be cursed.
29: zot torat haqin'ot: 'This is the law for cases of jealousy (qin'ah).' The formal legal summary term torat (law/instruction) marks the conclusion of the case. The sotah procedure has a legal name — 'the law of jealousy' — reflecting that jealousy (qin'ah) is the emotional and covenantal trigger that initiates the entire procedure. YHWH's own qin'ah (jealousy/passionate devotion) is the model; the husband's qin'ah mirrors it at the domestic level.
30: The summary restates both variants of the case: voluntary suspicion by the husband, and suspicion when 'the spirit of jealousy comes over him.' The two formulations acknowledge that jealousy has both deliberate and involuntary dimensions — it can be calculated accusation or overwhelming emotional response. The law covers both, placing both under the same divine arbiter. The ordeal is both a protection for the accused woman (against false accusation leading to mob violence) and an avenue for the jealous husband's resolution.
31: veniqqa ha'ish me'avon veha'ishah hahi tissa et avonah: 'The man will be free of guilt, and the woman will bear her own iniquity.' The legal summary's final statement has been interpreted variously. It means: if the ordeal is performed correctly and the verdict issues, the husband is legally cleared of any wrongful accusation charge (assuming he acted in good faith); the guilty woman bears her own sin through the physical consequences. The law is structured so that neither party is left in permanent legal limbo — the divine verdict resolves the impasse that human evidence cannot.
